questions.q: A static branch predictor always predicts backward branches (loops) as taken and forward branches (if-then) as not taken. On a program where 80% of backward branches are actually taken, what is the static predictor's accuracy on backward branches?
questions.explanation: If 80% of backward branches are taken and the static predictor predicts taken, it is correct 80% of the time on backward branches. This simple heuristic works well because loops are indeed usually taken.

questions.q: A dynamic branch predictor uses a branch history table (BHT) with 2-bit counters to track branch behavior. What does a 2-bit counter represent?
options: The number of times a branch was executed
Two bits representing four states: strongly not taken, weakly not taken, weakly taken, strongly taken
The address of the next instruction
The branch target address
questions.explanation: A 2-bit saturating counter uses two bits to encode a 4-state machine: 00 (strongly not taken), 01 (weakly not taken), 10 (weakly taken), 11 (strongly taken). This hysteresis prevents single misses from flipping predictions. The counter does not track execution counts or addresses.

questions.q: A branch has a 2-bit counter in state 11 (strongly taken). The branch is then not taken. Which state does the counter transition to?
options: 00 (strongly not taken)
10 (weakly taken)
01 (weakly not taken)
Remains at 11 (no change)
questions.explanation: A saturating counter transitions by one state per outcome. From 11, a not-taken outcome moves to 10 (weakly taken), not all the way to 00. This hysteresis ensures that one mispredict does not immediately flip the prediction.

questions.q: A branch target buffer (BTB) caches the addresses of taken branches. When a branch is fetched and found in the BTB, what information does the BTB provide?
options: The branch condition and register values
The target address, allowing the processor to fetch the next instruction without waiting for branch resolution
The prediction bit (taken or not taken)
The instruction count since the last branch
questions.explanation: The BTB stores the target addresses of taken branches, enabling early fetch of the target instruction. This reduces the branch latency and improves throughput. Prediction bits are stored in the BHT, not the BTB.

questions.q: A processor can issue 4 instructions per cycle. A branch misprediction occurs and 8 in-flight instructions must be flushed. How many cycles of lost throughput occur due to this misprediction?
options: 8 cycles
2 cycles
4 cycles
1 cycle
questions.explanation: 8 flushed instructions / 4-issue width = 2 cycles of lost throughput. This is the time needed to refill the pipeline after the flush completes and correct instruction fetching resumes.

questions.q: A branch predictor has an 8-entry branch target buffer (BTB). On a program with 16 distinct frequently-taken branches, what problem occurs?
options: The BTB will have no misses because 8 entries are sufficient for any workload
The BTB will experience capacity misses because 8 entries cannot hold all 16 branch targets simultaneously
The BTB must predict branch direction before checking for targets
BTB size is unrelated to branch count
questions.explanation: If 16 distinct branches compete for 8 BTB entries, some targets will be evicted due to capacity. On subsequent branch fetches, the BTB misses and must refetch the target address from memory, increasing latency. A larger BTB or fully associative design would reduce such misses.

questions.q: A pattern history table (PHT) predictor maintains global history of the last N branch outcomes and indexes into a table using this history. Why is global history useful for branch prediction?
options: Global history eliminates the need for any other prediction mechanism
Global history helps recognize correlated branches where the outcome of one branch influences subsequent branches
Global history only works for loops and nested conditionals
Global history is less accurate than a simple 2-bit counter
questions.explanation: Global history captures context: some branches behave differently depending on prior branches. For example, a branch in one path of an if-else may be taken more often than in another path. Local or per-branch history alone cannot model these correlations.

questions.q: A processor with a 64-entry BTB experiences a BTB miss on a branch that was previously cached but has been evicted. What is the penalty of this miss?
options: Zero cycles because the prediction mechanism still works without the BTB
Multiple cycles because the processor must access main memory to find the branch target address
One cycle, the standard cache miss latency
No penalty; the processor speculatively fetches and corrects if wrong
questions.explanation: A BTB miss requires fetching the target address, likely from instruction cache or memory, incurring latency before correct fetch direction is restored. Prediction (taken/not-taken) can proceed independently, but target address discovery is delayed.

questions.q: An if-else structure has a branch that is taken 90% of the time but the other 10% leads to an alternative path with a dependent branch. Which predictor type would struggle the most with the second (dependent) branch?
options: A global pattern history predictor that sees both branch outcomes and adapts
A static predictor that always predicts backward branches as taken
A 2-bit per-branch predictor that only tracks local branch history without global context
A BTB that caches branch target addresses
questions.explanation: A local (per-branch) 2-bit predictor sees the dependent branch in isolation. Since it only occurs in the 10% case, it may not accumulate enough history to predict accurately. A global predictor recognizes the context (the first branch outcome) and adapts. Static and BTB are orthogonal to this issue.

questions.q: A branch predictor with perfect accuracy (100% hit rate) would eliminate branch misprediction penalties entirely. However, perfect prediction is impossible. What fundamental reason prevents perfect branch prediction?
options: Branches depend on runtime data values determined dynamically at execution; predicting these values without executing them is impossible
Branch targets cannot be stored in the BTB due to hardware limitations
Perfect prediction requires infinite storage for all possible branch outcomes
Branches in loops always take the same outcome, making perfect prediction impossible
questions.explanation: Branches depend on register values, memory contents, and program input—all dynamic and unknown until execution. A predictor cannot see these values before executing instructions, making some misses inevitable. Infinite storage and loop outcomes are not the fundamental limitation.
